critic_raw:
The hypothesis claims a '15% improvement' in churn prediction accuracy but the primary metric is AUROC. AUROC and accuracy are different metrics; you cannot express a 15% AUROC improvement as a 15% accuracy improvement or vice versa. The design never reconciles this: the hypothesis speaks in accuracy terms, the evaluation uses AUROC, and the 15% threshold becomes unmeasurable against the chosen metric. A 15% relative improvement in AUROC from, say, 0.75 to 0.8625 is a very different claim than a 15% accuracy gain from 80% to 92%.

Sentiment features are derived from customer feedback text. Customer feedback is not randomly submitted—customers who submit feedback are a self-selected subpopulation, likely skewed toward dissatisfied users who are about to churn (complaint feedback) or very satisfied users (positive reviews). For the majority of non-churn and moderate-usage customers, no feedback exists. The design does not address how sentiment features are imputed for the 60-80% of users who likely submitted no feedback, yet these users' churn behavior is equally important. Mean-imputation or zero-imputation of sentiment scores for non-reviewers conflates 'no sentiment' with 'neutral sentiment,' which biases the model.

The stratified random split across 3 years of subscription data ignores churn seasonality (end-of-year, price increase events, competitor entry) and distributional shift. More critically, the monthly churn label means each user contributes multiple observations (one per month). Under random splitting, observations from the same user appear in both train and test, allowing the model to learn user-specific baseline churn propensity rather than generalizable patterns from sentiment and transaction signals.

The baseline is logistic regression on transactional data; the main model is gradient boosting with added sentiment features. This comparison conflates model architecture (logistic regression vs. gradient boosting) with feature enrichment (no sentiment vs. sentiment). Gradient boosting on transactional data alone would be a necessary intermediate condition to isolate whether the improvement comes from the model change or from the additional features.

Price changes and user behavior shifts are described as confounds addressed by 'incorporating relevant control features.' But price changes affect all users simultaneously and create a sharp discontinuity in the churn distribution at the time of the change. This is a structural break, not a gradual confound that can be controlled with a feature. Users who churn immediately after a price increase are different in mechanism from users who churn for other reasons; pooling them inflates the apparent predictability of churn from sentiment features.
all_issues_raised: The 15% improvement threshold in the hypothesis is stated in accuracy terms but the primary metric is AUROC; these are incommensurable, making the hypothesis claim unmeasurable against the chosen evaluation metric.

Sentiment features are only available for a self-selected subpopulation of users who submitted feedback; imputation for the majority with no feedback conflates 'no sentiment' with 'neutral sentiment,' biasing the model for the largest user segment.

Monthly churn labels for the same user across 3 years create multiple observations per user; a random split places the same user's observations in both train and test, allowing the model to learn user-level churn propensity rather than generalizable signals.

The comparison conflates model architecture (logistic regression vs. gradient boosting) with feature enrichment (no sentiment vs. sentiment); gradient boosting on transactional-only features is a missing intermediate condition.

Price changes create structural breaks in the churn distribution; pooling users who churned due to price increases with baseline churners and treating price period as a control feature misspecifies the causal mechanism.
